title: Query Execution
overview: Every query, whether it's a fast primary-key lookup or a slow five-way join, travels through the same mechanical pipeline before a single row comes back.
This page explains that pipeline end to end: connection pooling, parsing, optimization, plan execution, and how a plan turns into actual disk (or buffer pool) access.
This is deliberately about the mechanics of ANY query — the fixed stages every query passes through — not about diagnosing or speeding up a specific slow one.
For the practitioner skill of making a specific slow query faster, see `query-optimization`; for the index structures the optimizer chooses among, see `indexing`.
why: Understanding this pipeline is what separates "I write SQL" from "I understand what the database does with my SQL" — a common interview bar for backend and database-adjacent roles.
Without a model of this pipeline, it's hard to reason about why one query is instant and a structurally similar one is slow, or where a given optimization actually applies.
Knowing where time is actually spent (parsing vs planning vs disk I/O) is a prerequisite for reasoning about performance at all.
Concepts like buffer pool hits, execution plans, and connection reuse only make sense once you can place them on this pipeline.
Many production incidents (connection exhaustion, plan regressions, cold-cache slowdowns) map directly onto one specific stage of this flow.

intro: The stages below follow the pipeline in order: a query leaves the application, borrows a connection, gets parsed and planned by the database, and finally reads real data before the result streams back.
Every stage exists to solve one specific problem — skipping any one of them either breaks correctness (parsing) or makes performance unpredictable (optimizer, plan, disk access).

topics.heading: SQL Parser
points: The parser is the first thing that touches the raw SQL text arriving at the database — its job is purely syntactic and structural, not about performance yet.
It validates syntax (matching keywords, correct clause order, balanced parentheses) and rejects malformed SQL immediately with a syntax error.
It builds a **parse tree** (sometimes called an abstract syntax tree) — a structured, in-memory representation of the query's clauses, expressions, and references.
It also performs semantic checks at this stage: do the referenced tables and columns exist, are data types compatible, does the user have permission to run this statement.
Only a syntactically and semantically valid parse tree is handed forward to the optimizer — nothing about indexes or costs is considered yet.

topics.heading: Optimizer
points: The optimizer's job is to turn the parse tree into the cheapest available **execution strategy** — it does not change what the query means, only how it will be carried out.
It considers multiple candidate strategies: which index (if any) to use per table, which join algorithm to use, and in what order to join multiple tables.
Decisions are driven by **table statistics** the database maintains in the background — row counts, column value distributions, index cardinality — not by re-reading the actual data live.
Each candidate strategy is assigned an estimated **cost** (a unit-less number modeling estimated disk reads, CPU work, and memory use), and the lowest-cost plan is normally selected.

Stale statistics are a classic real-world failure mode here — if the optimizer's row-count estimates are far from reality (e.g., after a bulk load nobody analyzed), it can confidently pick a bad plan.

topics.heading: Execution Plan
points: The execution plan is the concrete, physical output of the optimizer's decision — a tree of operations the engine will actually carry out, in a specific order.
It fixes things the parse tree left open: exact table access order, the join algorithm per join (nested loop, hash join, merge join), and which index (or full scan) each table access uses.
This is what tools like `EXPLAIN` display — a human-readable rendering of that internal operation tree, not a separate thing from it.
The same query text can produce a different plan at different times if statistics change, data volume changes, or available indexes change.
Some databases cache and reuse plans for identical, parameterized queries to avoid re-planning on every execution — but a cached plan can go stale the same way statistics can.

topics.heading: Index Lookup & Disk Access
points: This is where the execution plan stops being a plan and starts touching real data — each operation in the plan issues page reads against storage.
Databases keep a **buffer pool** (an in-memory cache of recently used data pages) — a page read first checks the buffer pool; only a miss falls through to actual disk I/O.
A **buffer pool hit** is fast (memory-speed); a **buffer pool miss** requiring real disk I/O is orders of magnitude slower, especially on spinning disks and even measurably slower on SSDs.
An index lookup, mechanically, is a walk down the B+ Tree structure (see `indexing`) — each level visited is itself a page that may or may not already be cached.

A well-cached, frequently queried table can serve almost entirely from the buffer pool; a large, rarely touched table forces real disk reads on nearly every access — this is why the same query can be fast once and slow "cold".

topics.heading: Returning the Result
points: Once the plan's operations have produced matching rows, the engine assembles them into a **result set** in the shape the query requested (selected columns, sort order, row limit).
Results are typically **streamed** back to the client rather than fully materialized first — the application can often start consuming rows before the database has finished producing all of them.

The connection used for this query is then returned to the **connection pool** rather than closed, ready for reuse by the next request — avoiding the cost of establishing a brand-new database connection.

Any error at this stage (constraint violation mid-transaction, connection drop) has to be surfaced back through the same path, which is why result handling and error handling are tightly coupled to this last stage.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Application"] --> B["Connection Pool"]
    B --> C["Database"]
    C --> D["SQL Parser"]
    D --> E["Optimizer"]
    E --> F["Execution Plan"]
    F --> G["Index Lookup"]
    G --> H["Disk"]
    H --> I["Result"]
    I --> A
architectureFlow.caption: Every query follows this same fixed pipeline — the application borrows a pooled connection, the database parses and optimizes the SQL into a concrete plan, that plan drives index lookups down to disk (or buffer pool), and the result streams back through the same connection, which then returns to the pool.

comparisonTables.title: Pipeline Stage vs What Can Go Wrong There
rows: Pool exhaustion — requests queue waiting for a free connection
Syntax or reference errors surfaced immediately, before any data access
Picks the lowest-cost plan using fresh statistics
Stale statistics lead to a confidently wrong, expensive plan
Plan regression after a data or schema change invalidates old assumptions
Served from a warm buffer pool
Cold cache forces real disk I/O, dramatically increasing latency

simpleExample.scenario: Trace a single, simple primary-key lookup through the full pipeline, then see how the same pipeline behaves under a heavier, joined query as load increases.
simpleExample.code:
SELECT name, email FROM customers WHERE id = 501;

-- Under heavier load, a reporting query on the same pipeline:
SELECT c.name, o.id, o.total_amount
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.created_at >= '2026-07-01' AND o.created_at < '2026-07-19'
ORDER BY o.total_amount DESC;
simpleExample.input: An application request needing one customer's `name` and `email`, run against a `customers` table with a clustered index on `id`. Separately, the join query runs against an `orders` table with tens of millions of rows and a `customers` table with a few million, under a connection pool sized for typical traffic.
simpleExample.execution: For the simple lookup: the application borrows a connection from the pool. The parser validates the syntax and references, producing a parse tree. The optimizer sees an equality filter on the primary key and immediately favors the clustered index — no real alternative is worth costing out. The execution plan is a single clustered-index lookup, walking the B+ Tree down to a leaf, likely already warm in the buffer pool. For the join query under light load: a connection is available instantly, the optimizer picks an index range scan on `orders.created_at` followed by a lookup join into `customers`, and most pages are warm — the round trip is fast. Under heavy load: connections queue at the pool before the database is even involved, and if caches are cold after a deploy, the same plan now causes real disk reads instead of buffer pool hits.
simpleExample.output: The simple lookup returns one row in milliseconds. The join query returns a sorted result set, but its wall-clock time can vary by an order of magnitude between the light-load and heavy-load scenarios despite the execution plan itself being identical.
simpleExample.explanation: Even a query this simple passes through every stage of the pipeline — simplicity just means each stage does very little work, not that any stage is skipped. The join query shows the other side of the same lesson: a slow query isn't always a bad plan — it can just as easily be pool contention (before the database stage even starts) or a cold buffer pool (at the disk-access stage), which is exactly why `query-optimization` treats EXPLAIN output as only one diagnostic input among several.

interviewExplanation: Every query, no matter how simple, goes through the same fixed pipeline before a row ever comes back. The application first borrows an already-open connection from a pool rather than creating a fresh one — that's expensive to skip. Then the database's parser checks the SQL is syntactically and semantically valid and builds a parse tree. The optimizer takes that parse tree and, using background statistics like row counts and index cardinality, picks the cheapest execution strategy — which index to use, which join algorithm, what order to join tables in. That decision becomes a concrete execution plan, which is what `EXPLAIN` shows you.

From there, the plan actually touches data — it walks down an index structure and checks the buffer pool first before ever going to disk, because memory access is orders of magnitude faster. Finally, results stream back to the application and the connection returns to the pool for reuse.

The reason this pipeline view matters in interviews is that it gives you a structured way to diagnose 'why is this slow' instead of just blaming 'the database.' It could be pool exhaustion, a parsing issue, a bad plan from stale statistics, or simply a cold cache forcing real disk I/O — and each of those lives at a different, identifiable stage.

followUpQuestions.q: Why does every query need to go through a fixed pipeline instead of just running directly?
followUpQuestions.a: Each stage exists to solve a specific problem: the parser guarantees correctness before anything else happens, the optimizer picks an efficient strategy using statistics instead of guessing, and the buffer pool avoids paying disk cost repeatedly. Skipping any one of them either breaks correctness or makes performance unpredictable.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the difference between the parse tree and the execution plan?
followUpQuestions.a: The parse tree is a purely syntactic and semantic representation built by the parser, before any cost consideration. The execution plan is the optimizer's chosen physical strategy — table access order, join algorithms, index usage — built afterward using cost estimates.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the advantage of connection pooling in this pipeline?
followUpQuestions.a: It removes the cost of opening a brand-new database connection (network handshake, authentication, resource setup) on every single request by reusing already-open connections, which is why it sits as the very first stage before the database is even involved.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the trade-off or gotcha with the buffer pool specifically?
followUpQuestions.a: It makes performance state-dependent rather than purely plan-dependent — the exact same execution plan can be fast (buffer pool hit) or slow (buffer pool miss, real disk I/O) depending on whether the needed pages happen to be cached, which is why a query can be fast once and slow 'cold.'

followUpQuestions.q: When would this pipeline view NOT be the right lens to use?
followUpQuestions.a: When the problem is really about query correctness or business logic rather than performance — this pipeline explains the mechanics of executing any query, not whether the query itself is written correctly or optimally. For a specific slow query, `query-optimization` is the more targeted skill to apply.

followUpQuestions.q: What's a common mistake when diagnosing a slow request using this pipeline?
followUpQuestions.a: Assuming the execution plan itself is the problem without first ruling out connection pool queueing or a cold buffer pool — those live at completely different stages and require different fixes than a bad plan does.

bestPractices: Size the connection pool deliberately — an undersized pool queues requests before the database is even involved; an oversized one just adds contention on the database side.
Treat `EXPLAIN` as showing the optimizer's chosen plan, not a guarantee — corroborate with actual run-time metrics (`EXPLAIN ANALYZE`) when something feels off.
Keep table statistics current, especially after bulk loads or large deletes, since the optimizer's cost estimates are only as good as its statistics.
Distinguish application-side wait (pool contention) from database-side wait (parsing, planning, disk) when investigating a slow request end to end.
Design for cold-cache reality — the first request after a deploy or restart pays a disk-access cost that steady-state traffic doesn't.
commonMistakes: Assuming a query is slow because of a bad execution plan, without first ruling out connection pool queueing.
Never re-running statistics after major data changes, letting the optimizer plan against a stale picture of the table.
Treating a single `EXPLAIN` output as proof of steady-state performance, ignoring cold-cache vs warm-cache differences.
Closing and reopening a fresh connection per request instead of returning it to the pool, paying full connection-setup cost every time.
Conflating the parse tree with the execution plan — they are produced by different stages and serve different purposes.
summary: Every query passes through the same fixed pipeline: connection pool, parser, optimizer, execution plan, index/disk access, and result return.
The parser handles syntax and validity; the optimizer picks a cost-based strategy using statistics; the execution plan is that strategy made concrete.
This page covers the mechanics for any query — see `query-optimization` for diagnosing and fixing a specific slow one.

title: Query Optimization
overview: Query optimization is the practitioner skill of taking one specific slow query and making it fast — reading its execution plan, finding the expensive step, and fixing it.

Where `query-execution` explains the fixed pipeline every query travels through, this page is about diagnosis and technique: how to read what the database actually did, and how to change the query or its indexes so it does less work.
The core tool throughout is the execution plan itself, inspected via `EXPLAIN` (and `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`, which also runs the query and reports real timings).
This assumes familiarity with indexes — see `indexing` for the structures (including covering indexes) that optimization techniques rely on.
why: A query can be logically correct and still be extremely expensive — correctness and performance are independent concerns, and a query that's instant on a development machine with a thousand seed rows can be unusably slow in production against tens of millions.
Without inspecting the execution plan, it's guesswork whether a query is using an index, doing a full scan, or paying for an expensive sort or temporary table.

Most real slow-query problems come down to a small set of recurring patterns: a missed index, an unnecessary full scan, or a query shape that accidentally defeats an otherwise-good index — the fix is a repeatable diagnostic loop: read the plan, identify the expensive step, apply a targeted technique, then re-check the plan.
Being able to read an execution plan and act on it is one of the most consistently tested database skills in backend interviews.
Optimizing a query is far cheaper than over-provisioning hardware to compensate for one that's doing unnecessary work.

intro: The topics below form a diagnostic loop: start from the execution plan, understand the two scan strategies it might report, know the concrete signal a covering index leaves behind, understand what "cost" in a plan actually means, and finish with the recurring techniques that fix most slow queries.

topics.heading: Reading Execution Plans
points: `EXPLAIN` shows the plan the optimizer chose without running the query; `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` actually runs it and reports real row counts and timings alongside the plan.
The first things to check: scan type per table (index-based vs full scan), rows examined vs rows actually returned, which index (if any) was used, and any extra operations like sorting.
A large gap between rows examined and rows returned is the single strongest signal that a query is doing far more work than it needs to.
Because `EXPLAIN` alone reports estimates while `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` reports what actually happened, a large mismatch between the two is itself a signal — usually stale statistics.

topics.heading: Full Table Scan vs Index Seek
points: A **full table scan** reads every row in the table in physical order — shown as `type: ALL` (or `Seq Scan`) — and is expensive in proportion to table size regardless of how selective the filter is.
An **index seek** uses a sorted index structure to jump directly to the range of matching entries, examining far fewer rows when the filter is selective.
Seeing a full scan in a plan isn't automatically a bug — on a small table, or when a filter matches most of the table, a full scan can genuinely be the cheaper choice (see `indexing` for why).
The actionable version of this check: does the scan type match the expected selectivity of the filter? A full scan on a highly selective filter over a huge table is the pattern worth fixing.

topics.heading: Covering Index as an Optimization
points: A covering index (fully explained on the `indexing` page) removes an extra step from the plan: instead of an index lookup followed by a key lookup back to the table, the plan answers entirely from the index.
In a plan, this shows up as `Using index` with no separate row-fetch step — a concrete, checkable signal, not a guess.
As an optimization technique, this means: for a specific hot, repeated query, widening its existing index to include the columns it selects and sorts by can remove the key-lookup cost entirely.
This is a targeted technique, not a default — see `indexing` for the storage and write-cost trade-offs of adding columns to an index.

topics.heading: Understanding Query Cost
points: The **cost** value in a plan is a unit-less estimate the optimizer computes for each candidate strategy, modeling expected disk reads, CPU work, and memory use — not a direct measure of milliseconds.

Cost estimates are built from table statistics (row counts, value distribution, index cardinality); when those statistics are stale, cost estimates — and therefore the chosen plan — can be wrong even though nothing about the query changed.
Comparing the estimated cost (from `EXPLAIN`) against the actual measured time (from `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`) is how a stale-statistics problem gets caught in practice.
Lower cost is not always synonymous with fewer rows examined — a plan can have a low cost estimate and still be wrong if the underlying statistics don't reflect current data.

topics.heading: Common Optimization Techniques
points: **Avoid `SELECT *`** — fetch only needed columns to reduce I/O, memory, and network transfer, and to make covering indexes possible in the first place.
**Avoid functions on indexed columns** — `WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024` won't use an index on `created_at`; rewrite as a range: `WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01'`.
**Use `EXISTS` over `IN`** for large subqueries — `EXISTS` can short-circuit on the first match instead of materializing the whole subquery result.
**Batch operations** — batch inserts (`INSERT INTO table VALUES (...), (...), (...)`) to reduce round trips instead of issuing one statement per row.
**Re-run statistics after large data changes** — a bulk load or mass delete can invalidate the assumptions the optimizer's cost estimates depend on.
**Add or reshape an index based on the plan, not intuition** — every technique above should be confirmed by re-running `EXPLAIN` afterward, not assumed to have worked.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Slow query reported"] --> B["Run EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE"]
    B --> C{"Full scan on a selective filter?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Add or fix an index"]
    C -- "No" --> E{"Index used but still slow?"}
    E -- "Yes - many key lookups" --> F["Consider a covering index"]
    E -- "Yes - query shape defeats index" --> G["Rewrite query - remove function on column, EXISTS over IN"]
    D --> H["Re-run EXPLAIN to confirm"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Plan improved?"}
    I -- "No" --> B
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Done"]
architectureFlow.caption: Optimization is a loop, not a one-shot fix: read the plan, apply one targeted change, then re-read the plan to confirm it actually helped before moving on.

simpleExample.scenario: A lookup by email is slow, and the team wants to confirm why and fix it — then apply the same diagnostic loop to a heavier order-history query that's slow despite an index already existing.
simpleExample.input: A `customers` table with 2 million rows, filtered by `email`, with no index on that column. Separately, an `orders` table with tens of millions of rows, an existing single-column index on `customer_id`, queried under real production concurrency.
simpleExample.execution: The first `EXPLAIN` on `customers` shows `type: ALL`, `key: NULL`, and rows examined near 2 million — a full table scan. After creating the index, re-running `EXPLAIN` shows `type: ref`, `key: idx_customers_email`, and rows examined at 1. For the `orders` query, `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows the existing index narrows to one customer's orders, but still reports `Using filesort` (a separate sort for `ORDER BY created_at`) and a key lookup per row for `status` and `total_amount`, since those columns aren't in the index. After creating the wider composite index covering all four columns, re-running `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows the same narrowing, but now with `Using index` and no `Using filesort`.
simpleExample.output: The `customers` query now reports a plan using the new index instead of scanning the whole table. The `orders` query's measured execution time drops substantially, confirmed by comparing `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` timings before and after — not just by eyeballing the plan shape.
simpleExample.explanation: This is the core diagnostic loop applied twice, once simple and once compounded: don't just add an index and assume it worked — confirm the change in the plan itself. In the `orders` case, two savings stack — the composite index removes the full scan, and covering the remaining selected/sorted columns removes both the key lookup and the separate sort step.

interviewExplanation: Query optimization, to me, is a diagnostic loop, not guesswork. I always start by running `EXPLAIN`, or `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` if I want real timings and row counts instead of just estimates. The things I look for first are the scan type — is it using an index or doing a full table scan — and the gap between rows examined versus rows actually returned. A huge gap there is the strongest signal that the query is doing way more work than it needs to.

From there it's usually one of a small number of fixes: add or reshape an index, remove a function that's wrapping an indexed column and silently disabling it, swap `IN` for `EXISTS` on a large subquery, or avoid `SELECT *` so a covering index even becomes possible. The one rule I never skip is re-running `EXPLAIN` after every change — if the plan didn't actually change, the fix didn't work, no matter how confident I was that it would.

A full table scan by itself isn't automatically a problem, by the way — on a small table, or when the filter matches most of the rows anyway, a full scan can be genuinely cheaper than a bunch of scattered index lookups. The actionable question is always whether the scan type matches the filter's real selectivity.

followUpQuestions.q: Why is EXPLAIN the starting point rather than just guessing at a fix?
followUpQuestions.a: Because a query can be logically correct and still expensive — correctness and performance are separate concerns. Without inspecting the plan, you can't tell whether it's already using an index, doing a full scan, or paying for a hidden sort or temporary table, so any fix without that information is a guess.

followUpQuestions.q: What happens internally when you add a covering index to fix a slow query?
followUpQuestions.a: The plan changes from an index lookup followed by a separate key lookup back to the table, to answering entirely from the index itself — visible in the plan as `Using index` with no separate row-fetch step.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the advantage of EXPLAIN ANALYZE over plain EXPLAIN?
followUpQuestions.a: EXPLAIN only shows the optimizer's estimated plan and cost, without running the query. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes it and reports real row counts and timings, which is how you catch cases where the estimates — usually due to stale statistics — were wrong.

followUpQuestions.q: What's a common gotcha that silently disables an otherwise good index?
followUpQuestions.a: Wrapping the indexed column in a function, like `WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024` — the engine would have to compute the function for every row, so it falls back to a full scan. Rewriting it as a range (`created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01'`) restores index usage.

followUpQuestions.q: When should you NOT reach for a covering index?
followUpQuestions.a: When the query isn't actually hot or repeated enough to justify it — covering indexes add storage and slow down writes, so they're a targeted fix for high-traffic queries, not a default to apply everywhere.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the most common mistake people make when optimizing a query?
followUpQuestions.a: Adding an index (or making some other change) without confirming via EXPLAIN that it's actually being used, or trusting the estimated cost without ever checking EXPLAIN ANALYZE's real measured time — a fix that isn't confirmed in the plan is just a guess.

bestPractices: Always confirm a suspected slow query with `EXPLAIN` (or `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`) before changing anything — diagnose first, fix second.
Re-run the plan after every change to confirm it actually helped — a fix that isn't confirmed in the plan is just a guess.
Prefer rewriting the query (removing a function on an indexed column, switching `IN` to `EXISTS`) before reaching for a brand-new index, when a rewrite alone unlocks an existing one.
Reserve covering indexes for genuinely hot, repeated queries — see `indexing` for the storage and write-cost trade-offs.
Keep statistics fresh, especially after bulk loads, since stale statistics can make `EXPLAIN`'s cost estimates misleading.
commonMistakes: Adding an index without first confirming (via `EXPLAIN`) that the query would actually use it.
Trusting `EXPLAIN`'s estimated cost without ever checking `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`'s actual measured time.
Fetching every column with `SELECT *` and then wondering why a covering index isn't possible.
Wrapping an indexed column in a function and being surprised the optimizer falls back to a full scan.
Treating one optimization technique as a universal fix instead of matching the technique to what the plan actually shows.
summary: Query optimization is a repeatable loop: read the execution plan, identify the expensive step, apply a targeted fix, then re-read the plan to confirm it helped.
EXPLAIN shows the chosen plan and estimated cost; EXPLAIN ANALYZE additionally runs the query and reports real numbers.
A covering index and a well-chosen composite index remain the two most reliable structural fixes, on top of query rewrites.

title: Spring Transaction Integration
overview: Spring's `@Transactional` annotation lets application code declare transaction boundaries without writing manual `BEGIN`/`COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` calls.
Underneath, Spring wraps the annotated bean in an AOP proxy that starts a real database transaction before the method runs and commits or rolls it back after.
The mechanism is declarative, but it still ultimately drives the exact same database transaction concepts — Auto Commit, commit, rollback — covered on the Transactions page.
why: Hand-written transaction management means every service method repeats the same boilerplate — get a connection, disable Auto Commit, try the logic, commit on success, roll back on failure, release the connection — and it's easy to forget a rollback on one exception path or nest transactions incorrectly.
Spring's declarative model solves this by letting a single annotation express "this method's database work is one transaction," keeping business logic free of transaction plumbing.
Because it's proxy-based, the same annotation also handles propagation — deciding whether a method joins an existing transaction or starts an independent one — consistently across an entire codebase.
Centralizing the commit/rollback decision in a proxy removes the risk of a developer forgetting a rollback call in some exception path.
Understanding the proxy mechanism specifically matters because it's what explains the most commonly asked gotcha: self-invocation silently bypassing the transaction entirely.

intro: The mechanics of `@Transactional` come down to a proxy, a set of propagation rules for how nested transactional calls behave, and a handful of attributes controlling rollback, read-only optimization, and timeout. This is the same general AOP-proxy pattern Spring uses elsewhere (for example, method security), applied specifically here to database transaction management.

topics.heading: Spring @Transactional
points: **Propagation** — `REQUIRED` (default), `REQUIRES_NEW` (always starts new), `NESTED`, `SUPPORTS`, `NOT_SUPPORTED`.
**Rollback rules** — default: rollback on `RuntimeException`. Customize with `@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)`.
**Read-only** — `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` hints to JPA/the database and optimizes flush mode.
**Timeout** — `@Transactional(timeout = 30)` rolls the transaction back if it exceeds the timeout.
**Pitfall: self-invocation** — calling a `@Transactional` method from within the same class bypasses the proxy entirely.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Controller"] --> B["Service"]
    B --> C["Spring AOP Proxy"]
    C --> D["Transaction Manager"]
    D --> E["Connection"]
    E --> F["Auto Commit Disabled"]
    F --> G["Business Logic"]
    G --> H{"Commit or Rollback?"}
    H -- "Success" --> I["Commit"]
    H -- "Exception" --> J["Rollback"]
    I --> K["Connection Returned to Pool"]
    J --> K
architectureFlow.caption: A request's path through Spring's transactional proxy — the proxy intercepts the call before the real service method runs, hands off to the transaction manager to disable Auto Commit and open a real database transaction, then commits or rolls back based on how the business logic completes, finally returning the connection to the pool.

comparisonTables.title: REQUIRED vs REQUIRES_NEW Propagation
rows: Suspends it and starts a fresh one
Rolling back affects the whole shared transaction
Rolling back only affects the new, inner transaction
Independent operations, like audit logging that must persist even if the caller fails

simpleExample.scenario: A `CustomerService` method updates a customer's email address inside a single declarative transaction. A second, order-placement example shows the same mechanism rolling back a multi-step operation when a later step fails.
simpleExample.input: A valid `customerId` and a new email address for the first example. For the second, an `OrderRequest` for a product with insufficient stock remaining.
simpleExample.execution: Spring's proxy intercepts the call to `updateEmail`, asks the transaction manager to start a transaction (Auto Commit off) before the method body runs, then lets `findById` and `save` execute against that same transaction — if the method returns normally the proxy commits, if it throws the proxy rolls back. For `placeOrder`, `orderRepository.save()` runs first; if `reserveStock()` then throws because stock is insufficient, Spring triggers a rollback of the entire method, including the already-saved order.
simpleExample.output: The customer's email is updated and committed as a single transaction — a failure partway through leaves the email completely unchanged rather than partially applied. For the order flow, no order row persists in the database and no stock is reserved — the failed reservation rolled back the whole transaction, not just itself.
simpleExample.explanation: None of the actual `BEGIN`/`COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` logic appears in either method — it's entirely driven by the proxy wrapping the method, based on whether the method returns or throws. `rollbackFor = Exception.class` in the second example widens the default rollback rule (which only covers unchecked exceptions) to include checked exceptions too, so a checked `OutOfStockException` still triggers a full rollback.

interviewExplanation: `@Transactional` is Spring's way of letting me declare a transaction boundary instead of writing manual `BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, and `ROLLBACK` code. The mechanism behind it is an AOP proxy — Spring wraps the bean, and when I call an annotated method through that proxy, it asks the transaction manager to start a real database transaction, disabling Auto Commit, before my method body even runs. If the method returns normally, the proxy commits; if it throws — by default, an unchecked `RuntimeException` — the proxy rolls back everything.

The part I make sure to call out explicitly is the self-invocation pitfall, because it's one of the most commonly asked follow-ups. If I call a `@Transactional` method from another method in the same class using `this`, I'm not going through the Spring proxy at all — I'm calling the raw object directly. That means no transaction gets started, silently, with no error telling you it didn't work. The fix is to always call through a Spring-managed bean reference, not through `this` — for example, by injecting the bean into itself, or restructuring the call to go through a different bean entirely.

Beyond that, propagation controls what happens when a transactional method is called from within another transaction — `REQUIRED`, the default, just joins whatever transaction is already running, while `REQUIRES_NEW` suspends it and starts a completely independent one, which matters for something like audit logging that needs to survive even if the calling operation rolls back.

followUpQuestions.q: Why does @Transactional exist instead of just managing transactions manually?
followUpQuestions.a: Manual transaction management means repeating the same boilerplate in every service method — open a connection, disable Auto Commit, commit or roll back, release the connection — and it's easy to forget a rollback on some exception path. @Transactional centralizes that decision in a proxy, driven declaratively instead of by hand-written code in every method.

followUpQuestions.q: What actually happens internally when a @Transactional method is called?
followUpQuestions.a: The call goes through Spring's AOP proxy first, which asks the transaction manager to start a real database transaction (disabling Auto Commit) before the actual method body runs. If the method returns normally, the proxy commits; if it throws a matching exception, the proxy rolls back — none of that logic lives in the method itself.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the advantage of the declarative, proxy-based approach over manual transaction code?
followUpQuestions.a: It keeps business logic completely free of transaction plumbing, applies consistent propagation rules across the whole codebase, and removes the risk of a developer forgetting a rollback call on one exception path.

followUpQuestions.q: What's the most commonly asked disadvantage or gotcha with @Transactional?
followUpQuestions.a: Self-invocation — calling a @Transactional method from another method in the same class via `this` bypasses the Spring proxy entirely, so no transaction is started, silently and without any error. The fix is to always call through a Spring-managed bean reference.

followUpQuestions.q: When would you use REQUIRES_NEW instead of the default REQUIRED?
followUpQuestions.a: When an operation genuinely needs to be independent of the caller's transaction — the classic example is audit logging that must persist even if the calling business operation later rolls back. REQUIRES_NEW suspends the outer transaction and starts a fresh one that commits or rolls back on its own.

followUpQuestions.q: When should you avoid relying on the default rollback behavior?
followUpQuestions.a: Whenever a checked exception should still trigger a rollback — the default rule only covers unchecked RuntimeExceptions, so a checked exception (like a custom OutOfStockException) would otherwise let the transaction commit despite the failure, unless rollbackFor is set explicitly.

bestPractices: Keep `@Transactional` methods short and focused on the actual database work — long-running transactional methods hold locks and hurt concurrency, same as any long-running database transaction.
Use `readOnly = true` on query-only methods to let the persistence layer optimize.
Set `rollbackFor` deliberately whenever a checked exception should still trigger a rollback — don't rely on the default, which only covers unchecked exceptions.
Always call `@Transactional` methods through a Spring-managed bean reference, never via `this` from within the same class.
commonMistakes: Calling a `@Transactional` method from another method in the same class, silently bypassing the Spring proxy.
Assuming the default rollback rule covers checked exceptions — it only covers unchecked `RuntimeException`s by default.
Reaching for `REQUIRES_NEW` without considering that it suspends the outer transaction, which can mean a second physical connection is involved.
Placing `@Transactional` on a private method — Spring's proxy-based AOP can't intercept calls to private methods, so no transaction is applied.
summary: `@Transactional` lets Spring manage transaction boundaries declaratively, via an AOP proxy that starts, commits, or rolls back a real database transaction around the method call.
Propagation types (REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, NESTED, SUPPORTS, NOT_SUPPORTED) control how a transactional method behaves when called from within another transaction.
Rollback rules, readOnly, and timeout are configurable per method, but the proxy mechanism itself is what makes any of it possible — and what self-invocation silently bypasses.

title: Interview Questions
overview: This page collects the most important interview questions from across the entire Database section — architecture, objects, keys, design, normalization, indexing, transactions, isolation/locking, query execution, and Spring integration — organized by theme for final review.
Each question below has a concise model answer distilled from the deeper explanations on the individual topic pages. Use this to test recall quickly rather than re-reading full explanations.
This is a practice page, not a teaching page — if an answer doesn't fully make sense, go back to the corresponding topic page for the full reasoning.
why: Recognizing a concept while reading about it is a different skill from producing a clear, concise answer to it cold in an interview — practicing Q&A recall closes that gap.
Interviewers often chain follow-up questions off a short initial answer — rehearsing the concise version first makes it easier to expand confidently when pushed for more depth.
Grouping questions by theme mirrors how interviewers actually probe a candidate — a few questions per area rather than one deep dive on a single topic.

intro: Questions are grouped into five themes below, each with a compact model answer — read the question, try to answer it yourself first, then check the model answer.

topics.heading: Fundamentals & Architecture
points: **Q:** What is a database, and how is it different from a DBMS? — **A:** A database is the actual organized data; the `DBMS` (e.g. `PostgreSQL`, `MySQL`) is the software engine that manages, stores, and retrieves that data on the application's behalf.

**Q:** Why can't a large application just use a flat file instead of a database? — **A:** Flat files have no concurrency control (two writers can corrupt each other), no durability guarantees, and no query language — finding specific records means writing custom scanning code by hand.

**Q:** What does RDBMS stand for, and what makes it 'relational'? — **A:** Relational Database Management System — data lives in tables with a fixed schema of typed columns, and tables relate to each other through keys rather than duplicating data.

**Q:** Walk through what happens, layer by layer, when an application queries a database for the first time. — **A:** The call goes through a JDBC driver (translates to the DB's wire protocol), borrows a connection from a pool, reaches the database server, which asks the storage engine for the row; on a buffer pool cache miss it reads from disk and caches the page for next time.

**Q:** Why is a connection pool needed instead of opening a new connection per request? — **A:** Opening a raw connection costs a TCP handshake, authentication, and server-side setup — tens of milliseconds per request; a pool opens a fixed set once and lets requests borrow and return them instead.

**Q:** What is a buffer pool, and why can the same query be fast once and slow later? — **A:** It's an in-memory cache of recently used data pages inside the database server; a cache miss falls through to slow disk I/O and caches the page, so the next lookup of the same data is served from memory instead.

topics.heading: Design, Keys & Normalization
points: **Q:** What is a primary key, and why can't it be NULL? — **A:** It uniquely identifies every row in a table so any row can be targeted unambiguously; allowing NULL would mean some rows have no identity at all, breaking that guarantee.

**Q:** What is a foreign key used for, and how is it enforced? — **A:** It references the primary (or unique) key of another table to model a relationship; the database enforces referential integrity by checking a matching row exists on every insert/update, and applying the defined `ON DELETE` rule (`CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `RESTRICT`) when the referenced row is deleted.

**Q:** What is a composite key, and give an example where it's necessary. — **A:** A primary/unique key spanning two or more columns where no single column is unique alone but the combination is — e.g. `(order_id, product_id)` on an `order_items` table uniquely identifies one line item.

**Q:** On which side of a one-to-many relationship does the foreign key go? — **A:** Always on the 'many' side — e.g. `orders.customer_id` references `customers.id`, never the reverse, because a single column can only hold one reference per row.

**Q:** Why can't a many-to-many relationship be modeled with a plain foreign key, and what fixes it? — **A:** A single column can't hold multiple references per row; a **junction table** (e.g. `order_items`, linking `orders` and `products`) turns one N:N relationship into two 1:N relationships, and doubles as the home for relationship-specific attributes like `quantity`.

**Q:** What's the difference between 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF? — **A:** 1NF requires atomic values and no repeating groups; 2NF (on top of 1NF) removes partial dependencies on part of a composite key; 3NF (on top of 2NF) removes transitive dependencies, where a non-key column depends on another non-key column instead of directly on the primary key.

topics.heading: Indexing & Query Performance
points: **Q:** Why do indexes exist, and what do they cost? — **A:** Without one, the engine does a full table scan (`O(n)`) that gets slower as the table grows regardless of how few rows match; an index restructures the column into a sorted, searchable form for roughly `O(log n)` lookups, at the cost of extra storage and slower writes (every index must be updated too).

**Q:** What is a B+ Tree, and why is it used for indexes instead of a plain binary tree? — **A:** A balanced, sorted, multi-way tree where internal nodes only route the search (no row data) and leaf nodes hold the actual entries, linked together for fast range scans; its high fan-out keeps the tree shallow (3-4 levels even at millions of rows), unlike a binary tree which would need far more levels.

**Q:** What is the difference between a clustered and a non-clustered index? — **A:** A clustered index determines the physical row order on disk — the table data IS the leaf level, and there can be only one per table. A non-clustered index is a separate structure whose leaves point back to the row, so a table can have many, but each lookup pays an extra 'key lookup' step unless the index is covering.

**Q:** What makes an index a 'covering' index? — **A:** It contains every column a specific query needs (filter, sort, and selected columns) inside the index itself, so the engine answers entirely from the index without a trip back to the table — shown as `Using index` in the plan.

**Q:** What is the leftmost prefix rule for composite indexes? — **A:** A composite index on `(customer_id, status)` is sorted first by `customer_id`, then `status` — a query filtering on `customer_id` alone can use it, one filtering on both can use it fully, but one filtering on `status` alone cannot use it at all.

**Q:** What's the difference between `EXPLAIN` and `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`, and what does `type: ALL` mean? — **A:** `EXPLAIN` shows the optimizer's chosen plan and estimated cost without running the query; `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` actually executes it and reports real row counts and timings. `type: ALL` (or `Seq Scan`) means a full table scan — every row was read regardless of the filter's selectivity.

topics.heading: Transactions & Concurrency
points: **Q:** What does each letter in ACID stand for? — **A:** Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state to valid state, constraints enforced), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (once committed, survives crashes).

**Q:** What is Auto Commit, and why does it matter for multi-step operations? — **A:** Most databases default to Auto Commit, wrapping every single statement in its own transaction; a multi-step operation like a funds transfer needs Auto Commit turned off (or an explicit `BEGIN`) so both statements commit or roll back together, otherwise a crash between them leaves the data half-updated.

**Q:** What is the difference between a dirty read, a non-repeatable read, and a phantom read? — **A:** A dirty read sees another transaction's uncommitted change; a non-repeatable read sees a value change (already committed by another transaction) between two reads in the same transaction; a phantom read sees new rows appear that match a repeated query's filter.

**Q:** What is the difference between `READ_COMMITTED` and `REPEATABLE_READ`? — **A:** `READ_COMMITTED` (PostgreSQL's default) prevents dirty reads but still allows non-repeatable reads; `REPEATABLE_READ` (MySQL/InnoDB's default) additionally prevents non-repeatable reads by keeping a consistent snapshot for the whole transaction.

**Q:** What is a deadlock, and how does the database resolve it? — **A:** Two transactions each hold a lock the other needs, so neither can proceed; the database detects the circular wait automatically and aborts one transaction (the 'victim'), which the application must catch and retry. The real prevention is application-level: access rows in a consistent order across all code paths.

**Q:** How does MVCC let a plain `SELECT` avoid taking a read lock? — **A:** The database keeps multiple versions of each row and gives each transaction a consistent snapshot as of when it started; a reader sees the version committed at its snapshot time while writers create new versions instead of blocking the reader.

topics.heading: Spring & Production Integration
points: **Q:** What mechanism does Spring use under the hood to make `@Transactional` work? — **A:** Spring wraps the annotated bean in an AOP proxy that starts a real database transaction (disabling Auto Commit) before the method runs, then commits on normal return or rolls back on a matching exception.

**Q:** Why does self-invocation break `@Transactional`? — **A:** Calling a `@Transactional` method from another method in the same class goes through `this` directly, bypassing the Spring proxy entirely — so no transaction is started at all. The fix is to call through a Spring-managed bean reference (e.g. inject a self-reference or move the method to another bean).

**Q:** What's the difference between `REQUIRED` and `REQUIRES_NEW` propagation? — **A:** `REQUIRED` (the default) joins an existing transaction if one is active, so rolling back affects the whole shared transaction; `REQUIRES_NEW` suspends any existing transaction and starts an independent one, so its rollback doesn't affect the caller's transaction — useful for something like audit logging that must survive even if the main operation rolls back.

**Q:** Does the default `@Transactional` rollback behavior cover checked exceptions? — **A:** No — by default it only rolls back on unchecked `RuntimeException`s; a checked exception needs `@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)` to also trigger a rollback.

**Q:** Why doesn't `@Transactional` work on a private method? — **A:** Spring's proxy-based AOP can only intercept calls that go through the proxy (i.e. public method calls from another bean); a private method can't be proxied, so no transaction is ever started for it.

summary: These questions span the full Database section — from what a database is, through keys, normalization, and indexing, to transactions, concurrency, and Spring's transactional proxy.
Treat this as a rehearsal tool: cover the answer, read the question, answer it out loud, then check — repeat until every theme feels automatic.

title: Quick Revision
overview: This page condenses every topic in the Database section into short, one-line recall bullets — the last thing to skim before walking into an interview.

topics.heading: Architecture & Objects
points: Database = organized data; DBMS = the software managing it (e.g. PostgreSQL, MySQL).
Request path: Client to JDBC Driver to Connection Pool to Database Server to Storage Engine to Buffer Pool to Disk.
Buffer pool caches hot data pages in memory; a cache miss falls through to slow disk I/O.
Object hierarchy: Database contains Schemas, Schemas contain Tables, Tables contain Rows and Columns.
Five data type categories: numeric, string/text, date/time, boolean, binary — use DECIMAL for money, never floating-point.
Primary Key = unique row identifier, never NULL, enforced via an auto-created unique index; Foreign Key = enforced via a referential integrity check on write/delete.

topics.heading: Design & Normalization
points: Three relationship cardinalities: one-to-one (UNIQUE FK on either side), one-to-many (FK on the 'many' side), many-to-many (needs a junction table).
Junction table (e.g. order_items) resolves N:N into two 1:N relationships and holds relationship-specific attributes like quantity.
Prefer surrogate keys (auto-increment/UUID) over natural keys as primary keys, including on junction tables.
1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups. 2NF: 1NF + no partial dependency on part of a composite key. 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency through a non-key column.
BCNF tightens 3NF for the rare edge case of overlapping composite candidate keys — most schemas stop at 3NF.
Denormalize deliberately, only for measured read-heavy bottlenecks, and preferably in a separate reporting table, not the transactional schema.

topics.heading: Performance
points: Connection pooling reuses already-open connections instead of paying a TCP handshake + auth cost per request; close() on a pooled connection returns it to the pool, it doesn't actually close the socket.
A connection leak = borrowed connection never returned; leak-detection-threshold logs a stack trace to find it fast.
Full table scan cost grows with table size (O(n)); index lookup cost grows roughly with log(table size) via a B+ Tree.
Clustered index = physical row order, exactly one per table; non-clustered index = separate structure with pointers back to rows, many per table.
Composite index follows the leftmost prefix rule; a covering index answers a query entirely from the index (Using index, no key lookup).
Query pipeline: Connection Pool to SQL Parser to Optimizer to Execution Plan to Index Lookup/Disk to Result — EXPLAIN shows the plan, EXPLAIN ANALYZE also runs it and shows real timings.

topics.heading: Transactions & Concurrency
points: ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
Auto Commit (default): each statement is its own transaction; BEGIN/START TRANSACTION suspends it until COMMIT or ROLLBACK.
SAVEPOINT marks a point inside a transaction you can roll back to without losing earlier statements.
Isolation levels least to most strict: READ_UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads possible) to READ_COMMITTED (Postgres default) to REPEATABLE_READ (MySQL/InnoDB default) to SERIALIZABLE (strictest, slowest).
Shared lock: many concurrent readers. Exclusive lock: one writer, blocks everyone else. Row-level locking is the default for OLTP concurrency.
Deadlock = circular wait between transactions, resolved by aborting one as the victim; MVCC lets readers get a snapshot without blocking writers.

topics.heading: Spring Integration
points: @Transactional wraps a bean in an AOP proxy that starts, commits, or rolls back a real database transaction around the method call.
REQUIRED (default) joins an existing transaction; REQUIRES_NEW suspends it and starts an independent one.
Default rollback only covers RuntimeException — use rollbackFor = Exception.class to include checked exceptions.
Self-invocation (calling a @Transactional method via this from the same class) bypasses the proxy entirely, and @Transactional has no effect on private methods.

summary: Every bullet above traces back to a full explanation on its own topic page — use this only for final recall, not first-time learning.
